summary: Julia Evans catalogs the unwritten rules that terminal programs follow, despite there being no formal standards beyond POSIX and a few guidelines. She identifies seven conventions: Ctrl-C quits noninteractive programs, q quits TUIs, Ctrl-D quits REPLs, programs stick to 16 ANSI colors, readline keybindings are loosely supported, colors are disabled when piping, and - means stdin/stdout. She emphasizes these are descriptive observations from 20 years of terminal use, not prescriptions, and notes that understanding which rules are the program's responsibility helps explain why implementations vary slightly.
key_points: Terminal program behavior is governed by unwritten conventions rather than formal standards, yet programs behave surprisingly consistently
Many behaviors that feel like 'laws of terminal physics' (like Ctrl-D quitting a REPL) are actually explicitly implemented by each program to mimic OS defaults
Understanding whether a behavior comes from the OS, shell, terminal emulator, or program itself makes exceptions less surprising
Programs adapt their output format and color when writing to a pipe versus a terminal, which is a widely followed but uncodified convention
Learning these rules is a multi-stage process from basic awareness through subconscious pattern recognition to explicit understanding
money_quotes: As far as I know, there are no real standards for how programs in the terminal should behave.
Instead of 'uh, programs could do literally anything', it's 'ok, here are the basic rules I expect, and then I can keep a short mental list of exceptions'.

I actually thought that this one was a 'Law of Terminal Physics' until very recently because I've basically never seen it broken, but you can see that it's just something that each individual input library has to implement.
None of those programs actually uses readline directly, they just sort of mimic emacs/readline keybindings.
A lot of my understanding of the terminal is honestly still in the 'subconscious pattern recognition' stage.
The only reason I've been taking the time to make things explicit at all is because I've been trying to explain how it works to others.

If you stick to the default 16 base colours, you have a much better chance that the user has configured those colours in their terminal emulator so that they work reasonably well with their background color.
key_insight: Terminal programs follow a consistent set of unwritten conventions that feel like laws of nature but are actually explicitly implemented by each program, and making these implicit rules explicit accelerates learning for newcomers.
